title: RAPID-CDL
text: Welcome to the Reusable and Accessible Public Interest Documents project, part of ARDC’s Community Data Lab. The RAPID-CDL project aims to make public interest documents such as parliamentary proceedings, inquiry submissions, hearing transcripts, and ministerial records more accessible and usable for researchers across Australia.

title: About
text:
Public interest documents such as parliamentary proceedings, inquiry submissions, hearing transcripts, and ministerial records remain difficult for researchers to use because they are typically released as isolated, human‑readable files with inconsistent formats, gaps, and limited metadata, creating a “last‑mile” barrier that demands significant technical expertise and often prevents ambitious research. The RAPID-CDL project addresses this by making these documents more accessible, establishing a FAIR‑aligned metadata standard, and creating documented workflows for both computational and qualitative research.
Built on the Language Data Commons of Australia architecture, the project will apply RO‑Crate tools and the Oni portal to public interest collections, expanding their usability and informing future enhancements. The work will benefit a broad range of humanities, social sciences, Indigenous studies, and civil society users.
Expected outcomes include improved access to Federal Hansard and inquiry documents, a roadmap for extending coverage to all jurisdictions, searchable directories and indexes of inquiries and submissions, new research workflows, and sustainable practices for expanding public access to these vital records.

Partners
Project partners include the University of Queensland, Queensland University of Technology, and QCIF Digital Research.
This project is a co-investment partnership with the Australian Research Data Commons (ARDC) through the HASS and Indigenous Research Data Commons. The ARDC is enabled by the Australian Government’s National Collaborative Research Infrastructure Strategy (NCRIS).

title: Federal Parliament Speeches about the Job-Ready Graduates Bill
text:
What did Parliament actually say when it decided an Arts degree should cost up to $50,000?
We’ve just released a structured dataset of the Federal Parliamentary debate on the Job-ready Graduates Package, at a moment when there is a call for submissions to reverse the policy.
But before we rehearse the familiar arguments, it’s worth returning to the source. Often what is said in media commentary does not clearly match the practicalities of government. Before making assumptions, return to the source. It will ensure the strongest submission possible.
Our project extracts Federal Hansard debates from the Parliament of Australia and converts them into clean, research-ready datasets.
This one includes:

Every parliamentary reference to the Job-ready Graduates reforms
Full speech text (not excerpts)
Speaker metadata
Links back to the official transcripts

No scrolling through webpages. No copy-paste assembly. Just structured debate ready for analysis, teaching, journalism, or submission writing.
If you’re preparing a submission, conducting research, teaching higher education policy, or simply want to check the rhetoric against the record, this dataset gives you the debate in a usable form.
📎 Dataset link
📣 Call for submissions
We’re building an open project that makes parliamentary debate more accessible, searchable, and usable for researchers, educators, journalists and advocates.
If you use it, let us know, and cite the dataset! And if you’d like access to similar datasets on other debates, follow the project here.
